skills.skill: Advanced topics in Biology, Chemistry, and Physics (depending on student's chosen path)
skills.teks_description: Relate how past and current research impacts scientific thought and society
skills.prompt: This topic covers advanced science topics for 12th grade Science students (TEKS B.4(B)). Students should understand how past and current research impacts scientific thought and society. At the 12th grade level, students pursue specialized pathways in biology, chemistry, or physics, connecting classroom knowledge to current research frontiers. Advanced biology: CRISPR applications, epigenomics, microbiome research, neuroscience, bioinformatics. Advanced chemistry: computational chemistry, green chemistry, nanotechnology, materials science, drug design. Advanced physics: particle physics, cosmology, quantum computing, gravitational waves, fusion energy research. Key concepts: reading and interpreting primary research literature, understanding peer review, identifying research gaps, connecting fundamental science to technological applications, science communication skills. Students should evaluate how breakthrough discoveries (DNA structure, general relativity, quantum mechanics) transformed scientific paradigms. Vocabulary: paradigm shift, peer review, primary literature, research methodology, frontier research, interdisciplinary, translational research, innovation.

skills.teks_description: Analyze, evaluate, and critique scientific explanations using empirical evidence, logical reasoning, and experimental testing
skills.prompt: This topic covers research projects for 12th grade Science students (TEKS B.4(A)). Students should demonstrate mastery of scientific inquiry by designing and conducting independent research. Research process: identify a researchable question, conduct literature review, formulate testable hypothesis, design experimental methodology (controls, variables, sample size, statistical analysis plan), collect data, analyze results using appropriate statistical methods, draw evidence-based conclusions, communicate findings. Key concepts: research ethics (IRB protocols, informed consent, data integrity), experimental design principles, statistical analysis (t-tests, ANOVA, chi-square, correlation, regression), scientific writing (abstract, introduction, methods, results, discussion format), presentation skills. Students should understand the difference between experimental and observational studies, control for confounding variables, and assess the significance and limitations of their findings. Science fair participation or independent research mentorship with professionals provides authentic experience. Vocabulary: hypothesis, experimental design, control, variable, statistical significance, literature review, methodology, data analysis, conclusion, peer review, IRB, abstract.

skills.teks_description: Research STEM careers through museums, libraries, organizations, companies, and mentors
skills.prompt: This topic covers independent study in science for 12th grade students (TEKS B.4(C)). Students should research STEM careers through museums, libraries, professional organizations, companies, and mentors. This self-directed learning experience allows students to explore a scientific topic of personal interest in depth. Key components: selecting a focused topic, developing a research plan with timeline and milestones, working with a mentor (teacher, university professor, industry professional), maintaining a research journal, producing a final product (paper, presentation, portfolio, prototype). Career exploration: identifying STEM career pathways, understanding educational requirements, gaining awareness of professional skills needed, networking with professionals. Resources: scientific journals (Nature, Science, PNAS), databases (PubMed, Google Scholar), professional organizations (ACS, APS, AAAS), university partnerships, internship opportunities. Key concepts: self-directed learning, time management, professional communication, developing expertise in a specialized area, connecting classroom learning to real-world applications. Vocabulary: independent study, mentor, research plan, STEM career, professional development, portfolio, primary source, database, internship, networking.

skills.skill: AP or IB level courses in science
skills.teks_description: Advanced Placement or International Baccalaureate coursework; extends beyond standard TEKS
skills.prompt: This topic covers AP and IB level science courses for 12th grade students. These advanced courses extend beyond standard TEKS, providing college-level rigor. AP courses: AP Biology (molecular biology, genetics, ecology, evolution at college depth), AP Chemistry (thermodynamics, equilibrium, kinetics, electrochemistry with quantitative emphasis), AP Physics C (calculus-based mechanics and electricity/magnetism), AP Environmental Science (earth systems, resources, pollution). IB courses: Higher Level (HL) courses require deeper understanding and additional topics compared to Standard Level (SL); Internal Assessment (IA) requires original experimental investigation. Key concepts: college-level problem solving, laboratory skills, extended response writing, independent research, preparation for college placement exams. Students should develop skills in quantitative analysis, scientific argumentation, experimental design, and critical reading of scientific literature. These courses prepare students for success in college-level science and competitive STEM programs. Vocabulary: AP exam, IB assessment, college credit, laboratory investigation, free response, multiple choice, internal assessment, extended essay, college readiness.

skills.skill: Specialized science courses (e.g., anatomy, physiology, astronomy, environmental science, forensic science)
skills.teks_description: TEKS Chapter 112 Subchapter C includes specialized HS science electives
skills.prompt: This topic covers specialized science electives for 12th grade students (TEKS §112.C). Students can pursue in-depth study in specific scientific disciplines. Anatomy and Physiology: detailed study of human body structure and function; organ systems, tissue types, pathophysiology; preparation for healthcare careers. Astronomy: study of celestial objects, stellar evolution, cosmology, observational techniques; connects physics to space science. Environmental Science: ecosystems, sustainability, pollution, natural resources, environmental policy; interdisciplinary approach. Forensic Science: application of science to law; evidence collection and analysis, DNA fingerprinting, toxicology, ballistics, crime scene investigation. Additional electives: Aquatic Science, Earth and Space Science, Engineering Design. Key concepts: applying core science principles to specialized contexts, developing career-relevant skills, understanding interdisciplinary connections between sciences. These courses provide hands-on, applied learning that connects theoretical knowledge to real-world professional practice. Vocabulary: specialization, elective, applied science, interdisciplinary, career pathway, laboratory technique, field study, case study.

skills.teks_description: Relate how past and current research impacts scientific thought and society
skills.prompt: This topic covers science-technology-society connections for 12th grade students (TEKS B.4(B)). Students should understand how scientific research impacts technology development and societal change. Historical examples: Pasteur's germ theory transformed medicine and public health; Haber-Bosch process enabled synthetic fertilizers (feeding billions but causing environmental damage); discovery of DNA structure led to biotechnology revolution; transistor invention enabled computer revolution. Current connections: AI and machine learning, gene editing (CRISPR ethical debates), climate science and policy, renewable energy technology, pandemic response and vaccine development, space exploration (Mars missions, commercial spaceflight). Key concepts: technology as applied science, unintended consequences of technological development, science policy and funding, public understanding of science, responsible innovation, science communication. Students should analyze how societal needs drive scientific research and how scientific discoveries create new technological capabilities and social challenges. Vocabulary: innovation, technology transfer, unintended consequences, science policy, responsible research, science communication, public engagement, ethical implications, societal impact.

skills.teks_description: Relate how past and current research impacts scientific thought and society including ethical considerations
skills.prompt: This topic covers ethics in science for 12th grade students (TEKS B.4(B)). Students should understand ethical considerations in scientific research and their societal implications. Research ethics: honesty and integrity in data reporting, proper attribution and avoiding plagiarism, informed consent for human subjects (IRB review), humane treatment of animal subjects (IACUC review), conflicts of interest disclosure. Bioethics: genetic engineering debates (germline vs. somatic editing, designer babies), stem cell research, cloning, end-of-life decisions, organ transplantation allocation. Environmental ethics: anthropocentrism vs. ecocentrism, intergenerational responsibility, environmental justice (disproportionate impacts on marginalized communities), precautionary principle. Dual-use research: research with potential for both beneficial and harmful applications (gain-of-function virology, nuclear physics, AI). Key concepts: scientific integrity, responsible conduct of research, whistleblowing, data fabrication/falsification, scientific misconduct consequences, balancing innovation with precaution. Vocabulary: bioethics, research ethics, informed consent, IRB, IACUC, plagiarism, conflict of interest, dual-use, precautionary principle, environmental justice, scientific misconduct.

skills.teks_description: Relate how past and current research impacts scientific thought and society
skills.prompt: This topic covers current events in science for 12th grade students (TEKS B.4(B)). Students should connect current scientific developments to foundational principles and evaluate their societal significance. Current areas of rapid advancement: artificial intelligence and machine learning (large language models, autonomous vehicles, drug discovery), gene editing (CRISPR clinical trials for sickle cell disease, cancer immunotherapy), climate science (IPCC reports, tipping points, carbon capture technologies, renewable energy breakthroughs), space exploration (James Webb Space Telescope discoveries, Mars missions, commercial spaceflight, asteroid mining), pandemic preparedness (mRNA vaccine technology, antiviral development, genomic surveillance), quantum computing (quantum supremacy claims, practical applications). Key skills: reading and evaluating science news (distinguishing reliable sources from misinformation), understanding peer review process, recognizing media bias in science reporting, evaluating claims using evidence-based reasoning. Students should regularly read science news (Nature, Science, Scientific American) and discuss implications. Vocabulary: peer review, preprint, meta-analysis, scientific consensus, science journalism, misinformation, evidence-based, breakthrough, clinical trial, paradigm.

skills.teks_description: Research STEM careers and preparation through organizations, companies, and mentors
skills.prompt: This topic covers preparation for college science for 12th grade students (TEKS B.4(C)). Students should develop skills and knowledge needed for success in college-level STEM courses. Academic skills: reading primary scientific literature, scientific writing (lab reports, research papers), quantitative reasoning, problem-solving strategies, time management, study skills specific to science courses. Laboratory skills: proper technique, safety protocols, equipment use, data collection and recording, error analysis, lab report writing. Mathematical preparation: calculus applications in physics and chemistry, statistical analysis, graphing and data visualization, dimensional analysis, unit conversions. Career awareness: exploring STEM fields through research, internships, and mentorship; understanding educational pathways (bachelor's, master's, PhD, professional degrees); connecting coursework to career goals. College transition: understanding expectations differences (pace, independence, depth), developing self-advocacy skills, utilizing office hours and tutoring, collaborative learning. Key resources: AP and IB exam preparation, SAT Subject Tests, college application guidance, scholarship opportunities, summer research programs (REU). Vocabulary: undergraduate, STEM, research experience, laboratory skills, scientific literacy, academic writing, study skills, mentorship, internship, career pathway.

skills.teks_description: TEKS Chapter 112 Subchapter C: Specialized HS science - Earth and Space Science standards
skills.prompt: This topic covers astronomy for 12th grade Science students (TEKS §112.C). Students should understand celestial objects, their properties, and the methods used to study them. Observational astronomy: telescopes (refracting, reflecting, radio), electromagnetic spectrum observations, space-based observatories (Hubble, James Webb). Stellar astronomy: stellar classification (spectral types OBAFGKM), Hertzsprung-Russell diagram (main sequence, giants, supergiants, white dwarfs), stellar evolution (protostar → main sequence → red giant → planetary nebula/supernova → white dwarf/neutron star/black hole). Solar system: formation (solar nebula hypothesis), planetary types (terrestrial, gas giants, ice giants), moons, asteroids, comets, Kuiper Belt, Oort Cloud. Exoplanets: detection methods (transit, radial velocity, direct imaging), habitable zone, search for biosignatures. Key concepts: light-year as distance unit, redshift and expanding universe, spectroscopy for composition analysis, gravitational effects on celestial motion. Vocabulary: telescope, spectroscopy, luminosity, magnitude, main sequence, red giant, white dwarf, neutron star, black hole, exoplanet, habitable zone, light-year, parallax.

skills.teks_description: TEKS Chapter 112 Subchapter C: Earth and Space Science - origins and structure of the universe
skills.prompt: This topic covers cosmology for 12th grade Science students (TEKS §112.C). Students should understand the origin, structure, and evolution of the universe. Big Bang theory: universe began ~13.8 billion years ago from an extremely hot, dense state; evidence includes cosmic microwave background radiation (CMB, discovered 1965), Hubble's law (galaxies recede proportionally to distance: v = H0d), abundance of light elements (hydrogen, helium predicted by Big Bang nucleosynthesis). Universe evolution: initial expansion and cooling → nucleosynthesis (first 3 minutes) → recombination (380,000 years, CMB released) → dark ages → first stars and galaxies → large-scale structure formation. Dark matter: ~27% of universe; does not emit light but affects gravity; evidence from galaxy rotation curves, gravitational lensing, CMB anisotropies. Dark energy: ~68% of universe; causes accelerating expansion; discovered 1998 via Type Ia supernovae. Universe geometry: flat (critical density), open, or closed; observations suggest flat. Future: depends on dark energy behavior (heat death, Big Crunch, Big Rip). Vocabulary: Big Bang, cosmic microwave background, Hubble's law, redshift, dark matter, dark energy, nucleosynthesis, expansion, inflation, cosmological constant, multiverse.

skills.teks_description: Provide examples of atomic and nuclear phenomena applications using the standard model
skills.prompt: This topic covers astrophysics for 12th grade Science students (TEKS Phy.9(D)). Students should apply physics principles to understand astronomical phenomena. Stellar physics: nuclear fusion in stellar cores (proton-proton chain: 4H → He + energy), stellar energy balance (radiation pressure vs. gravitational collapse), stellar luminosity (L = 4πR²σT⁴), mass-luminosity relation (L ∝ M^3.5 for main sequence). Stellar endpoints: white dwarfs (electron degeneracy), neutron stars (neutron degeneracy, pulsars), black holes (Schwarzschild radius rs = 2GM/c², event horizon, singularity). General relativity: gravity as spacetime curvature, gravitational lensing, gravitational waves (detected by LIGO 2015 from merging black holes), GPS corrections. High-energy astrophysics: cosmic rays, gamma-ray bursts, active galactic nuclei, quasars. Compact objects: accretion disks, jets, X-ray binaries. Key equations: escape velocity, orbital mechanics, Doppler effect for radial velocity, parallax for distance measurement. Vocabulary: nuclear fusion, main sequence, white dwarf, neutron star, black hole, event horizon, gravitational wave, general relativity, spacetime curvature, pulsar, quasar, accretion disk.

skills.teks_description: TEKS Chapter 112 Subchapter C: Earth and Space Science - planetary systems
skills.prompt: This topic covers planetary science for 12th grade Science students (TEKS §112.C). Students should understand the formation, composition, and processes shaping planetary bodies. Solar system formation: solar nebula hypothesis — collapse of gas/dust cloud → rotating disk → accretion of planetesimals → protoplanets → clearing of debris. Planetary types: terrestrial (Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars — rocky, dense, thin atmospheres) vs. Jovian (Jupiter, Saturn — gas giants; Uranus, Neptune — ice giants). Planetary interiors: differentiation (heavier elements sink), core, mantle, crust structure. Atmospheres: composition depends on mass, temperature, distance from sun, volcanic activity; greenhouse effect (Venus extreme, Earth moderate, Mars minimal). Surface processes: volcanism, tectonics, erosion, impact cratering, weathering. Comparative planetology: why Earth is habitable (liquid water, magnetic field, moderate greenhouse effect, plate tectonics). Moons and small bodies: tidal heating (Io, Enceladus, Europa), ring systems, asteroids, comets, KBOs, dwarf planets. Exoplanets: thousands discovered; habitable zone, biosignatures, SETI. Vocabulary: accretion, differentiation, terrestrial planet, gas giant, atmosphere, greenhouse effect, habitability, tidal heating, magnetosphere, exoplanet, biosignature.

skills.teks_description: TEKS Chapter 112 Subchapter C: Environmental Systems or Aquatic Science standards
skills.prompt: This topic covers environmental science for 12th grade Science students (TEKS §112.C). Students should understand environmental systems and evaluate human impacts on the natural world. Earth systems: atmosphere, hydrosphere, geosphere, biosphere — interconnected and interdependent. Key environmental issues: climate change (greenhouse gas emissions, global warming, sea-level rise, extreme weather), biodiversity loss (habitat destruction, overexploitation, pollution, invasive species, climate change), water resources (freshwater scarcity, pollution, groundwater depletion, ocean acidification), energy (fossil fuel dependence, transition to renewables, nuclear considerations), waste management (solid waste, hazardous waste, e-waste, plastic pollution). Sustainability: meeting present needs without compromising future generations; ecological footprint analysis; circular economy vs. linear economy. Environmental policy: major legislation (Clean Air Act, Clean Water Act, ESA, NEPA), international agreements (Paris Agreement, Montreal Protocol). Risk assessment and cost-benefit analysis. Solutions: green technology, conservation strategies, sustainable agriculture, urban planning. Vocabulary: sustainability, ecosystem services, ecological footprint, greenhouse effect, biodiversity, eutrophication, carrying capacity, renewable energy, environmental policy, conservation.

skills.teks_description: TEKS Chapter 112 Subchapter C: Earth and Space Science - geologic processes
skills.prompt: This topic covers geology for 12th grade Science students (TEKS §112.C). Students should understand Earth's structure, composition, and dynamic processes. Earth's structure: crust (oceanic basalt, continental granite), mantle (convection currents), outer core (liquid iron, generates magnetic field), inner core (solid iron). Plate tectonics: lithospheric plates move on asthenosphere; divergent boundaries (mid-ocean ridges, rift valleys), convergent boundaries (subduction zones, mountain building, volcanic arcs), transform boundaries (earthquakes, San Andreas Fault). Evidence: continental fit, fossil distribution, paleomagnetic reversals, seafloor spreading, GPS measurements of plate motion. Rock cycle: igneous (crystallization from magma/lava), sedimentary (compaction and cementation of sediments), metamorphic (heat and pressure transform existing rocks); continuous transformation. Geologic time: relative dating (superposition, cross-cutting relationships), absolute dating (radiometric: half-life calculations), geologic time scale (Eons, Eras, Periods, Epochs). Earth resources: minerals, fossil fuels, groundwater; sustainable extraction. Natural hazards: earthquakes, volcanic eruptions, landslides, tsunamis. Vocabulary: plate tectonics, subduction, convection, rock cycle, igneous, sedimentary, metamorphic, radiometric dating, geologic time, earthquake, volcano, mineral, fossil, stratigraphy.

skills.teks_description: TEKS Chapter 112 Subchapter C: Earth and Space Science - atmospheric science
skills.prompt: This topic covers meteorology for 12th grade Science students (TEKS §112.C). Students should understand atmospheric science, weather patterns, and climate systems. Atmosphere layers: troposphere (weather, temperature decreases), stratosphere (ozone layer), mesosphere, thermosphere, exosphere. Energy budget: solar radiation, albedo, greenhouse effect, radiation balance. Weather factors: temperature, pressure, humidity, wind, precipitation, cloud formation. Air masses and fronts: cold fronts (sharp weather change), warm fronts (gradual change), stationary fronts, occluded fronts. Pressure systems: low pressure (rising air, clouds, precipitation, counterclockwise in NH), high pressure (sinking air, clear skies, clockwise in NH). Severe weather: thunderstorms (convective, requires instability, moisture, lift), tornadoes (rotating thunderstorms, Fujita scale), hurricanes (tropical cyclones, warm ocean water, Saffir-Simpson scale). Global circulation: Hadley, Ferrel, Polar cells; Coriolis effect; trade winds, westerlies, polar easterlies; jet streams. Climate vs. weather: climate is long-term average; climate zones (tropical, temperate, polar); climate change evidence and models. Vocabulary: atmosphere, front, air mass, pressure system, Coriolis effect, jet stream, convection, precipitation, hurricane, tornado, climate, weather, albedo, greenhouse effect.

skills.teks_description: TEKS Chapter 112 Subchapter C: Aquatic Science standards
skills.prompt: This topic covers oceanography for 12th grade Science students (TEKS §112.C). Students should understand ocean systems, their properties, and their role in Earth's environment. Ocean structure: surface zone (warm, mixed by waves), thermocline (rapid temperature decrease), deep zone (cold, dense). Seawater properties: salinity (~35 ppt), temperature, density, dissolved gases, pH. Ocean circulation: surface currents (driven by wind, deflected by Coriolis effect, e.g., Gulf Stream), thermohaline circulation (deep water formation driven by density differences from temperature and salinity, global conveyor belt), upwelling (nutrient-rich cold water rises, high productivity). Waves and tides: wave properties (wind-generated), tsunamis (seismic), tides (gravitational pull of Moon and Sun, spring and neap tides). Ocean floor: continental shelf, slope, rise, abyssal plain, mid-ocean ridges, trenches, hydrothermal vents. Marine ecosystems: coral reefs, open ocean, deep sea, estuaries, intertidal zone; primary productivity by phytoplankton. Human impacts: overfishing, pollution (plastics, oil spills, chemical runoff), ocean acidification, coral bleaching, dead zones. Vocabulary: salinity, thermocline, thermohaline circulation, upwelling, current, tide, continental shelf, abyssal plain, mid-ocean ridge, coral reef, phytoplankton, ocean acidification.

skills.teks_description: TEKS Chapter 112 Subchapter C: Forensic Science course standards
skills.prompt: This topic covers forensic science for 12th grade Science students (TEKS §112.C). Students should understand the application of scientific principles to legal investigations. Crime scene investigation: securing the scene, documentation (photography, sketching, notes), evidence collection (chain of custody), evidence preservation. Physical evidence analysis: fingerprints (latent, patent, plastic; classification by loops, whorls, arches), DNA analysis (STR profiling, PCR amplification, CODIS database), blood evidence (typing, bloodstain pattern analysis, presumptive tests), trace evidence (hair, fibers, glass, soil, pollen), firearms and tool marks (ballistics, rifling, comparison microscopy). Chemical analysis: toxicology (drug and poison detection, blood alcohol), arson investigation (accelerant detection by gas chromatography), drug identification. Biological evidence: entomology (insect succession for time of death), anthropology (skeletal analysis for identification), serology, microbiology. Digital forensics: data recovery, metadata analysis. Key concepts: Locard's exchange principle (every contact leaves a trace), scientific method applied to investigation, expert witness testimony, limitations and reliability of forensic methods. Vocabulary: forensic science, evidence, chain of custody, fingerprint, DNA profiling, ballistics, toxicology, autopsy, Locard's principle, presumptive test, confirmatory test, expert witness.

skills.teks_description: TEKS Chapter 130 Subchapter K: CTE STEM and Engineering courses
skills.prompt: This topic covers engineering for 12th grade Science students (TEKS §130.K). Students should understand the engineering design process and explore various engineering disciplines. Engineering design process: identify the problem, research and gather information, brainstorm solutions, select the best solution, build a prototype, test and evaluate, redesign and iterate. Engineering disciplines: mechanical (machines, mechanisms, thermodynamics), electrical/electronic (circuits, power systems, signal processing), civil (structures, transportation, water systems), chemical (chemical processes, materials, pharmaceuticals), biomedical (medical devices, prosthetics, tissue engineering), aerospace (aircraft, spacecraft, propulsion), computer/software (algorithms, systems, AI), environmental (pollution control, sustainability). Key concepts: constraints (budget, time, materials, safety, environmental), trade-offs, optimization, systems thinking, failure analysis, factor of safety, ethical responsibilities. STEM skills: CAD design, prototyping (3D printing), coding, data analysis, teamwork, technical communication. Engineering connects science and mathematics to practical problem-solving for society. Vocabulary: engineering design process, prototype, iteration, constraint, trade-off, optimization, specification, tolerance, factor of safety, systems thinking, CAD, simulation.

skills.teks_description: Computer Science II (§126.34) or Computer Science III (§126.35) advanced topics
skills.prompt: This topic covers advanced computer science for 12th grade Science students (TEKS §126.34/§126.35). Students should understand advanced programming concepts, data structures, algorithms, and computational thinking. Data structures: arrays, linked lists, stacks, queues, trees (binary, BST, balanced), hash tables, graphs, heaps. Algorithms: sorting (merge sort O(n log n), quicksort, bubble sort O(n²)), searching (binary search O(log n), linear search O(n)), graph algorithms (BFS, DFS, Dijkstra's, A*), dynamic programming, greedy algorithms. Algorithm analysis: Big O notation for time and space complexity, best/average/worst case analysis. Advanced topics: object-oriented programming (classes, inheritance, polymorphism, encapsulation), recursion, database design (SQL, relational model, normalization), networking basics (TCP/IP, HTTP), cybersecurity fundamentals, AI and machine learning concepts (supervised/unsupervised learning, neural networks). Software development: version control (Git), testing, debugging, documentation, Agile methodology. Key concepts: abstraction, modularity, computational complexity, problem decomposition. Vocabulary: data structure, algorithm, complexity, Big O, recursion, object-oriented, class, inheritance, database, SQL, API, version control, machine learning, neural network.
